heroOverview: Cervicalgia (ICD-10: M54.2) is pain localised to the cervical region of the spine, spanning the muscles, joints, ligaments, discs, and nerve roots of the neck from the base of the skull to the upper thoracic junction. Neck pain is the fourth leading cause of years lived with disability globally, affecting an estimated 30–50% of adults in any given year and resulting in more than 100 million U.S. physician visits annually. Most episodes are mechanical in origin — arising from posture strain, muscle overuse, or degenerative change — and resolve within four to six weeks. However, approximately 50% of people who have one episode of neck pain report persistent or recurrent symptoms at one year, making it one of the most prevalent chronic musculoskeletal conditions managed in primary care, physiotherapy, and orthopaedic practice.

keyStats.prevalence: 30–50% of adults experience neck pain in any given year; global point prevalence approximately 14% (GBD 2016 data)
keyStats.demographics: Peaks in working-age adults aged 35–65; women report higher rates than men in most surveys by a ratio of approximately 1.3:1
keyStats.avgAge: Most common in the fourth to sixth decade; onset can occur at any age from adolescence onward
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 332 million people worldwide affected at any one point in time (GBD 2016)

definition: Cervicalgia (ICD-10: M54.2) is pain arising from the cervical spine and its supporting structures, including the intervertebral discs, facet joints, ligaments, muscles, and nerve roots of C1 through C7-T1. The condition is broadly classified as mechanical (non-specific) neck pain, which accounts for 85–90% of cases and arises from postural strain, muscle overuse, degenerative disc or facet disease, or minor soft-tissue injury without a specific structural lesion; and specific neck pain, which has an identifiable cause such as disc herniation with radiculopathy, cervical myelopathy, inflammatory arthropathy, fracture, tumour, or infection. Acute cervicalgia is defined as pain lasting under six weeks; subacute as six to twelve weeks; and chronic as persisting beyond twelve weeks. Symptom referral to the head (cervicogenic headache), shoulders, and scapular region is common because of the shared dermatomal and myotomal innervation. Neck pain is typically managed within orthopaedics, sports medicine, or neurology depending on the underlying cause, with physiotherapy as a cornerstone of treatment for the majority of cases. The socioeconomic burden is substantial — neck pain is a leading cause of work disability and healthcare utilisation in high-income countries.

typesClassification.description: The most common form, with no identified structural pathology; caused by muscle strain, postural overload, or minor facet joint irritation. Self-limiting in most patients over four to six weeks.
Pain arising from age-related disc degeneration, osteophyte formation, and facet joint arthrosis in the cervical spine; common in adults over 40 and often recurrent or chronic.

Neck pain combined with arm radiation, numbness, or weakness from cervical nerve root compression, most often at C6 or C7 level; requires targeted investigation and frequently epidural or surgical treatment.
Referred pain from cervical joints or muscles that presents predominantly as a unilateral headache, often confused with migraine; responds to cervical physiotherapy or facet joint injections.
Cervicalgia following rapid acceleration-deceleration neck injury, typically in road traffic accidents; grades I–IV according to the Quebec Task Force classification based on neurological findings.

Neck pain from ankylosing spondylitis, rheumatoid arthritis, or other inflammatory arthropathy; characterised by morning stiffness lasting over an hour, elevated inflammatory markers, and bilateral or axial pattern.

primarySymptoms: Dull, aching, or throbbing pain localised to the posterior or lateral neck, rated mild to severe, that may be constant or provoked by movement.
Neck stiffness limiting range of motion, particularly rotation and lateral flexion, that is most pronounced in the morning or after prolonged static posture.
Pain referred to the occiput, temples, or forehead (cervicogenic headache) occurring alongside the neck pain.
Pain radiating to the shoulder, scapular region, or upper arm when adjacent nerve roots or referred muscle trigger points are involved.
Muscle tightness or visible spasm in the upper trapezius, levator scapulae, or suboccipital muscle groups palpable on examination.
Tenderness of the posterior cervical facet joints or paraspinal muscles on palpation.
A catching or clicking sensation in the neck on rotation or extension, reflecting facet joint or disc-related movement impairment.
Pain that worsens with looking up (extension), turning the head rapidly, or maintaining a fixed forward-head posture for prolonged periods.
Disrupted sleep from difficulty finding a comfortable head position, particularly in chronic cervicalgia with disc or facet involvement.
Fatigue and reduced concentration associated with constant neck pain and sleep disruption.
earlyWarningSigns: Awareness of neck stiffness or discomfort after prolonged desk or screen work that resolves with position change.
Increasing frequency of morning neck stiffness that takes longer than usual to ease.
Recurrent mild occipital headaches starting at the base of the skull.
Neck discomfort that is now present at rest as well as with movement.
emergencySigns: Neck pain after significant trauma (vehicle collision, fall from height, sport impact) — do not move the person; immobilise the neck and call emergency services.
Neck pain with progressive arm or leg weakness, loss of hand dexterity, or difficulty with walking — these suggest cervical myelopathy and require urgent MRI and neurosurgical assessment.
New severe neck pain with fever, night sweats, and weight loss, raising concern for spinal infection or malignancy.
Sudden severe 'thunderclap' neck and head pain — this requires emergency imaging to exclude subarachnoid haemorrhage.
Neck pain with bladder or bowel dysfunction, indicating possible cervical cord compression requiring emergency evaluation.

causes.cause: Postural muscle overload and forward-head posture
causes.description: For every centimetre the head moves forward from neutral alignment over the shoulders, the effective load on the cervical spine approximately doubles; chronic screen use, desk work, and smartphone use commonly drive forward-head posture that fatigues and eventually injures the posterior cervical musculature and posterior ligamentous complex.

causes.cause: Degenerative disc and facet joint disease
causes.description: With age, cervical intervertebral discs lose hydration and height, transmitting greater loads to the facet joints; osteophytes develop around narrowed disc spaces and can irritate nerve roots or the spinal cord; this process — cervical spondylosis — is present radiologically in over 85% of adults above age 60 and is the most common structural cause of chronic cervicalgia.

causes.cause: Muscle strain and acute soft-tissue injury
causes.description: Sudden forceful neck movements, lifting with poor technique, prolonged awkward sleeping posture, or direct impact cause micro-tearing of cervical muscle fibres and ligaments, producing localised inflammation and protective muscle spasm that evolves into neck pain over hours.

When the nucleus pulposus of a disc herniates posterolaterally, it compresses an adjacent nerve root in the intervertebral foramen, producing neck pain combined with a radicular pattern of arm pain, numbness, and weakness; C5-C6 and C6-C7 are the most commonly affected levels.

Rapid forced extension and flexion of the cervical spine, most often in rear-end vehicle collisions, causes microtears in muscles, ligaments, and facet joint capsules; the resulting pain and stiffness can persist for months to years in a subset of patients even without identifiable structural damage on imaging.

Rheumatoid arthritis can erode the atlantoaxial joint causing instability; ankylosing spondylitis causes cervical fusion and stiffness; both conditions produce neck pain with a distinct inflammatory character — symmetrical, worse in the morning, and associated with elevated ESR and CRP.

riskFactors.factor: Prolonged screen or computer use with forward-head posture
riskFactors.description: Office workers spending more than four hours daily at a screen have significantly higher neck pain prevalence; the risk is compounded by non-ergonomic workstation setup.
Repetitive overhead lifting, carrying heavy loads on the shoulders, and vibration exposure increase cervical disc and muscle loading, raising risk of both acute and chronic neck pain.
A history of cervical trauma increases the risk of chronic cervicalgia by two to three times, likely through persistent sensitisation and structural change.
Psychological factors are among the strongest predictors of transition from acute to chronic neck pain; they increase central sensitisation and pain perception and correlate with greater disability.
Smoking accelerates cervical disc degeneration by impairing disc nutrition via the vertebral end-plates and increases the risk of both neck pain and radiculopathy by approximately 30–50%.
Cervical spondylosis prevalence rises steeply with age; women report neck pain at higher rates than men across most age groups.
Sleep disturbance and chronic neck pain are bidirectional; poor sleep reduces pain thresholds and impairs cervical muscle recovery from daily loading.

riskFactors.factor: Sedentary lifestyle and low physical fitness
riskFactors.description: Low neck and shoulder muscle endurance increases the load placed on passive cervical structures; regular exercise reduces recurrence risk by approximately 30% in observational studies.

affectedDemographics: Working-age adults aged 35–65 in desk-based occupations — the highest-prevalence group
Manual workers including construction, nursing, and agriculture, where physical loading of the neck is high
Adolescents and young adults in higher-education settings with prolonged laptop and smartphone use
Postmenopausal women with osteoporosis and associated disc degeneration
Athletes in contact sports and overhead sports (gymnastics, volleyball, wrestling)
Adults with pre-existing inflammatory arthropathy including ankylosing spondylitis and rheumatoid arthritis
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of cervicalgia starts with a structured history: onset, duration, character, radiation, precipitating events, red flag symptoms (fever, unintentional weight loss, progressive neurological deficit, bladder or bowel change, history of cancer), and psychosocial factors that influence prognosis. Physical examination includes inspection and palpation of the cervical spine for muscle spasm, tenderness, and deformity; cervical range-of-motion measurement; and a neurological upper-limb screen including grip strength, biceps, brachioradialis, and triceps reflexes, and sensation in dermatomes C5–T1. In non-specific neck pain without neurological signs or red flags, imaging is not required at first presentation and does not improve outcomes. MRI of the cervical spine is the investigation of choice when radiculopathy, myelopathy, tumour, or infection is suspected; it visualises disc herniations, nerve root compression, cord signal change, and soft-tissue abnormalities without radiation. CT is preferred for suspected fracture or when MRI is contraindicated. Plain X-rays are used to assess alignment and age-related spondylotic change. Nerve conduction studies and electromyography localise and characterise radiculopathy. Blood tests including ESR, CRP, FBC, and tumour markers are ordered when inflammatory or malignant causes are suspected. Psychological assessment tools such as the Neck Disability Index and the STarT MSK tool identify patients at risk of chronicity and guide early intervention.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measure degrees of flexion, extension, rotation, and lateral flexion to quantify restriction and monitor response to treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The examiner asks you to move your head in each direction while a goniometer or inclinometer measures the angle; takes 5–10 minutes and is generally well tolerated.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detect cervical radiculopathy or myelopathy through reflex testing, sensation mapping, and grip strength measurement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard neurological examination involving reflex hammers, light touch testing, and grip dynamometry; takes about 15 minutes and is not painful.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard imaging for evaluating disc herniation, nerve root or spinal cord compression, infection, malignancy, and inflammatory arthropathy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in a scanner for 30–45 minutes; no ionising radiation; a contrast injection may be added for infection or tumour assessment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assess alignment, fracture, instability, and degree of age-related spondylosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standing and lateral X-rays; takes a few minutes; involves low-dose ionising radiation.

diagnosticTests.test: CT scan of the cervical spine
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detailed bony imaging for complex fractures, bone tumour, or cases where MRI is contraindicated.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Three-minute scan in a ring-shaped machine; no preparation; higher radiation than X-ray but fast and highly detailed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirm the level and severity of cervical radiculopathy when imaging and clinical findings are inconsistent.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small electrical stimuli are applied to the arm and fine needles are placed in muscles; mild discomfort; takes 30–60 minutes with a neurophysiologist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screen for inflammatory arthropathy, infection, or malignancy when red-flag symptoms are present alongside neck pain.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw from the arm; results available in hours to one day.

treatmentOverview: Most cervicalgia resolves with conservative management. First-line treatment for acute mechanical neck pain is staying active, reassurance that serious pathology is absent, and a short course of analgesics. NICE and international guidelines recommend paracetamol plus an NSAID for acute cervicalgia; muscle relaxants such as diazepam or cyclobenzaprine are used for severe spasm over one to two weeks. Opioids are not recommended for non-specific neck pain. Physiotherapy is the cornerstone of both acute and chronic management: manual therapy (mobilisation and manipulation of cervical and thoracic joints), therapeutic exercise, and postural correction consistently outperform passive treatments alone in randomised trials. Exercise programmes focusing on cervical and scapular stabilising muscles reduce recurrence by approximately 30–50% in people with chronic neck pain. For cervical radiculopathy, initial conservative management with physiotherapy and NSAIDs resolves symptoms in more than 80% of patients within six to twelve weeks without surgery. Cervical epidural steroid injections reduce acute radicular arm pain when conservative treatment alone is insufficient. Surgery — anterior cervical discectomy and fusion or cervical disc arthroplasty — is reserved for radiculopathy or myelopathy that fails conservative treatment or where neurological function is threatened. Psychological approaches including cognitive behavioural therapy and mindfulness significantly improve outcomes in chronic pain and are particularly important when yellow-flag psychosocial factors are identified early.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (naproxen, ibuprofen, diclofenac)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line pharmacological treatment for acute and subacute mechanical cervicalgia; reduce inflammation and prostaglandin-mediated pain signalling in the cervical musculature and joints; used for two to four weeks at recommended doses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Short-term pain reduction compared to placebo in approximately 60–70% of patients with acute neck pain; effects are modest and clinically meaningful.

medicalTreatments.name: Paracetamol (acetaminophen)
medicalTreatments.description: Combined with an NSAID as first-line analgesia; used when NSAIDs are contraindicated due to renal impairment, peptic ulcer, or cardiovascular risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest analgesia for mild-to-moderate neck pain; less effective than NSAIDs alone but valuable in combination therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Muscle relaxants (cyclobenzaprine, diazepam)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-term use (one to two weeks maximum) for acute cervicalgia with prominent muscle spasm; central mechanism of action reduces spasm intensity and improves short-term comfort.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provide additional pain and spasm relief in approximately 50–60% of acute cases; not recommended for chronic use due to dependence risk.

medicalTreatments.name: Cervical epidural steroid injection
medicalTreatments.description: Fluoroscopy-guided injection of corticosteroid into the epidural space at the level of nerve root compression; reduces radicular arm pain in cervical radiculopathy by suppressing local inflammation around the compressed nerve root.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinically meaningful short-term reduction in arm pain in approximately 60–75% of patients with cervical radiculopathy; effect is transient over weeks to months.

medicalTreatments.name: Antidepressants (amitriptyline, duloxetine)
medicalTreatments.description: Low-dose tricyclic antidepressants or SNRIs are used in chronic cervicalgia with neuropathic features or when sleep disruption and central sensitisation are prominent; modulate descending pain inhibitory pathways.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduction in chronic pain intensity and improved sleep in approximately 40–50% of patients with central sensitisation component.

surgicalOptions.name: Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF)
surgicalOptions.description: The affected disc is removed through a small anterior neck incision and the vertebral bodies are fused with a cage and plate; the standard surgical treatment for cervical radiculopathy and myelopathy failing conservative management.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Arm pain relief achieved in approximately 80–90% of patients at two years; patient satisfaction rates above 80% in most series.

surgicalOptions.name: Cervical disc arthroplasty (artificial disc replacement)
surgicalOptions.description: An artificial disc prosthesis is inserted after discectomy to preserve motion at the operated level, avoiding the accelerated degeneration adjacent to fusion that occurs in a subset of ACDF patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Non-inferior to ACDF for radiculopathy at two-year follow-up; motion preservation confirmed in approximately 70–80% of patients at five years.

surgicalOptions.name: Posterior cervical foraminotomy
surgicalOptions.description: A keyhole posterior approach enlarges the intervertebral foramen to decompress a nerve root without fusion; preferred for lateral disc herniation and foraminal stenosis when the spine is stable.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Arm pain resolution in approximately 85–90% of appropriately selected patients with foraminal stenosis; lower morbidity than anterior approaches.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cervical and thoracic manual therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Physiotherapist-applied joint mobilisation and manipulation improves cervical range of motion and reduces pain in acute and subacute neck pain; evidence from randomised trials supports combination with exercise over either alone.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: Multiple systematic reviews find acupuncture provides short-term pain reduction superior to sham acupuncture in chronic neck pain, though the effect size is modest and durability beyond three months is uncertain.

alternativeTreatments.name: Heat and cryotherapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Application of heat (heat packs, warm shower) reduces cervical muscle spasm in the acute phase; ice reduces inflammation in the first 48 hours after acute soft-tissue injury; both are useful adjuncts to analgesics.

alternativeTreatments.name: TENS (transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation)
alternativeTreatments.description: Surface electrodes applied to the neck deliver low-level electrical current that may activate inhibitory pain pathways; evidence is modest but TENS is safe and some patients report meaningful short-term relief.

whySeeSpecialist: An orthopaedic spine specialist or neurosurgeon should be consulted when neck pain is accompanied by progressive arm weakness, hand clumsiness, or gait disturbance suggesting myelopathy, or when radicular symptoms fail six weeks of conservative treatment. A rheumatologist is appropriate when an inflammatory arthropathy is suspected. Any neck pain with red-flag features — fever, unexplained weight loss, cancer history, or pain that is constant and unresponsive to rest or analgesics — warrants urgent specialist review.
doctorSelectionGuide: For uncomplicated mechanical neck pain, a physiotherapist experienced in cervical manual therapy and exercise prescription is the most effective first point of specialist contact. For cervical radiculopathy or myelopathy, seek an orthopaedic spine surgeon or neurosurgeon who performs cervical arthroplasty and ACDF, and who uses validated outcome measures. For inflammatory arthropathy suspected as the cause, a rheumatologist with spine expertise should lead management. Ask the physiotherapist whether they combine manual therapy with supervised exercise — this combination shows the best evidence for long-term recurrence reduction.
preventionStrategies: Optimise workstation ergonomics — position the monitor at eye level and maintain elbows at desk height to keep the cervical spine in neutral.
Take active movement breaks every 30–45 minutes during prolonged sitting or screen use, including chin-tuck and gentle rotation exercises.
Strengthen cervical and scapular stabilising muscles through regular exercise; a physiotherapy-designed programme reduces recurrence by approximately 30–50%.
Sleep on a supportive pillow that maintains cervical neutral alignment and avoid sleeping prone.
Manage psychosocial stressors proactively — anxiety and work-related distress are major predictors of acute-to-chronic transition.
Stop smoking, which measurably accelerates cervical disc degeneration.
Use hands-free devices for prolonged phone calls rather than shoulder-cradling the phone.
lifestyleModifications: Adjust screen position and chair height so that the monitor top is level with your eyes and the keyboard allows a neutral wrist and shoulder posture.
Practice daily gentle cervical range-of-motion exercises — rotation, lateral flexion, and retraction — to maintain mobility and reduce muscle tightening.
Use a supportive cervical pillow for sleep; replace pillows that have lost shape or support.
Apply moist heat for 15–20 minutes to the posterior neck before exercise or physiotherapy to improve tissue extensibility.
Avoid cradling a phone between the ear and shoulder or holding the head in a fixed forward position for extended calls.
Incorporate swimming or upper-body strengthening into weekly routine; these consistently show the most benefit for recurrent neck pain prevention.

recommended: Anti-inflammatory diet including oily fish (omega-3 fatty acids), colourful vegetables, and olive oil to support disc and joint health
Adequate calcium and vitamin D for skeletal and muscle health, particularly in postmenopausal women with cervical spondylosis
Sufficient protein intake to support cervical muscle repair and conditioning
Generous hydration to support intervertebral disc fluid content
avoid: Smoking — accelerates cervical disc degeneration more than any dietary factor
Excess alcohol, which impairs sleep quality and worsens central pain sensitisation
Highly processed, high-sugar foods that promote systemic inflammation contributing to disc degeneration

exerciseGuidelines: Regular exercise is one of the most effective long-term strategies for reducing neck pain recurrence. Cervical stabilisation exercises — specifically deep neck flexor activation (chin tuck) and scapular retraction exercises — improve segmental control of the cervical spine. Strength training of the upper back and shoulder girdle distributes load away from the neck. Aerobic exercise including swimming, cycling, and walking at least 150 minutes per week reduces systemic inflammation and improves pain thresholds. High-impact contact sports and overhead lifting with heavy loads should be modified during acute flares. Yoga and Pilates programmes emphasising cervical neutral posture have demonstrated modest benefit in randomised trials for chronic cervicalgia.
dailyManagement: Perform prescribed physiotherapy exercises consistently, ideally once in the morning and once in the evening; adherence predicts long-term benefit.
Track daily pain scores and identify aggravating postures or activities so you can adjust them; patterns often reveal modifiable work or leisure triggers.
Use heat on the neck before activity and ice after if inflammation flares — apply through a cloth for 15–20 minutes maximum.
Do not carry bags or backpacks unilaterally on the shoulder for extended periods; use a backpack or distribute load evenly.
If neck pain is preventing sleep, trial a cervical pillow with appropriate loft for your sleep position (higher for side sleepers, lower for back sleepers).
prognosis: The majority of acute cervicalgia episodes — approximately 70–80% — resolve within four to six weeks with or without treatment. However, the long-term outlook is less uniformly optimistic: approximately 50% of people who experience neck pain report persistent or recurrent symptoms at twelve months. Chronic cervicalgia (lasting beyond three months) carries a poorer prognosis for complete resolution and is associated with significant quality-of-life and occupational disability. Early psychosocial yellow-flag screening and activation of physiotherapy substantially improves long-term outcomes. Cervical radiculopathy treated conservatively resolves in 80–90% of cases within three to six months. Cervical myelopathy, if diagnosed before significant cord damage has occurred, stabilises in the majority of surgically treated patients, though deficits present before surgery frequently persist.
recoveryTimeline: Most acute mechanical cervicalgia improves substantially within two to four weeks and resolves within six weeks. Whiplash-associated cervicalgia resolves within three months in approximately 50% of patients and within one year in approximately 80%. Cervical radiculopathy resolves or becomes manageable with conservative treatment in four to twelve weeks in most cases. Recovery from anterior cervical discectomy and fusion is typically two to four weeks before returning to light activity, with full return to manual work in six to twelve weeks. Chronic cervicalgia does not follow a linear recovery timeline; a structured physiotherapy and exercise programme typically shows meaningful improvement at eight to twelve weeks and continues for six or more months.
complications: Cervical radiculopathy — nerve root compression causing arm pain, numbness, and weakness that can become permanent if untreated.
Cervical myelopathy — spinal cord dysfunction from canal stenosis causing hand clumsiness, gait disturbance, and possible paralysis if decompression is delayed.
Chronic pain syndrome with central sensitisation, affecting approximately 15–20% of neck pain sufferers and associated with depression, sleep disruption, and work disability.
Cervicogenic headache — a frequent complication of chronic cervical spine and soft-tissue pathology, often misdiagnosed and undertreated as primary headache.
Adjacent segment degeneration after cervical fusion, accelerating disc disease at levels above or below the fusion over five to ten years.
Psychological sequelae including anxiety and depression, present in approximately 25–35% of people with chronic disabling cervicalgia.

supportResources.name: NHS — Neck Pain
supportResources.description: Plain-English UK patient information on neck pain causes, self-care, when to seek help, and treatment options.

supportResources.name: NASS — Cervical Conditions Patient Information
supportResources.description: North American Spine Society patient education resources on cervical spine conditions and treatment options.

supportResources.name: NICE — Neck Pain (Non-specific) Evidence Guidance
supportResources.description: NICE guideline NG59 on low back pain and sciatica with parallel recommendations for neck pain management.

sources.title: Hoy DG et al. The global burden of neck pain: estimates from the Global Burden of Disease 2010 study. Annals of the Rheumatic Diseases, 2014
Gross A et al. Manipulation and mobilisation for neck pain contrasted against an inactive control or another active treatment. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2015
Cohen SP. Epidemiology, diagnosis, and treatment of neck pain. Mayo Clinic Proceedings, 2015
NICE NG59 — Low back pain and sciatica in over 16s: assessment and management (cervical guidance appended)
Hurwitz EL et al. Treatment of neck pain: noninvasive interventions — results of the Bone and Joint Decade 2000–2010 Task Force on Neck Pain. Spine, 2008
Nikolaidis I et al. Surgery for cervical radiculopathy or myelopathy. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2010

confusedWithConditions.name: Migraine
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Migraine is a primary headache disorder with nausea, photophobia, and throbbing pain; cervicogenic headache from cervicalgia starts in the neck and radiates forward, lacks photophobia, and responds to cervical treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fibromyalgia causes widespread musculoskeletal pain at multiple sites including the neck, plus fatigue and sleep disruption; cervicalgia is localised to the cervical region without the diffuse body pain pattern.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ankylosing spondylitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ankylosing spondylitis causes inflammatory back and neck pain with sacroiliac involvement, elevated CRP, and HLA-B27 positivity; mechanical cervicalgia lacks these inflammatory markers and axial features.

confusedWithConditions.name: Torticollis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Torticollis is specifically characterised by a tilted and rotated head posture due to muscle contraction; cervicalgia is generalised neck pain without the fixed abnormal posture.

relatedConditions.name: Cervical radiculopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: A frequent complication of cervical spondylosis and disc herniation causing cervicalgia, adding arm pain and neurological deficits to the neck pain.

relatedConditions.name: Torticollis
relatedConditions.relevance: Torticollis causes neck pain as a core symptom and is often initially misidentified as simple cervicalgia until the characteristic head tilt is appreciated.

relatedConditions.name: Whiplash injury
relatedConditions.relevance: Whiplash-associated disorder is a major cause of both acute and chronic cervicalgia, accounting for a large proportion of neck pain presentations in emergency and primary care.

relatedConditions.name: Frozen shoulder
relatedConditions.relevance: Shoulder pain and restricted movement from frozen shoulder is frequently referred to the neck, coexists with cervicalgia, and must be distinguished from purely cervical pathology.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoarthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Cervical facet joint osteoarthritis is a major structural cause of chronic cervicalgia, particularly in adults over 50.

faqs.question: What is cervicalgia?
faqs.answer: Cervicalgia is the medical term for neck pain arising from the muscles, joints, discs, or nerve roots of the cervical spine. It covers everything from short-lived muscle strain to chronic pain from disc degeneration or nerve compression.

faqs.question: What causes neck pain?
faqs.answer: The most common causes of neck pain are muscle strain from poor posture or awkward sleep position, age-related disc and facet joint degeneration, and minor soft-tissue injuries. Less frequently, a herniated disc compressing a nerve root, inflammatory arthritis, or — rarely — infection or cancer cause neck pain.

faqs.question: How long does neck pain last?
faqs.answer: Most acute neck pain episodes resolve within two to six weeks. About half of people who have neck pain still report symptoms at twelve months, particularly if pain was severe at onset or psychosocial stress is present. Structural causes such as disc herniation or spondylosis can produce recurring or chronic pain lasting months to years.

faqs.question: When is neck pain serious?
faqs.answer: Neck pain becomes serious when it follows significant trauma, when it is accompanied by arm or leg weakness or numbness, or when there is fever, unexplained weight loss, or difficulty controlling the bladder or bowel. These symptoms can indicate nerve compression, spinal cord injury, infection, or malignancy and need urgent medical assessment.

faqs.question: Can neck pain cause headaches?
faqs.answer: Yes. Neck pain frequently causes cervicogenic headache — pain that originates from the cervical spine joints and muscles and is referred to the back of the head and temples. It is often one-sided, starts in the neck, and worsens with certain neck positions. It responds to cervical physiotherapy, unlike migraine.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for neck pain?
faqs.answer: For most acute neck pain, staying active combined with NSAIDs and physiotherapy gives the best short-term relief. For chronic neck pain, a supervised exercise programme focusing on cervical stabilising muscles and manual therapy is the strongest evidence-based approach, with better long-term outcomes than passive treatments or medication alone.

faqs.question: Does neck pain go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Most acute mechanical neck pain — from sleeping awkwardly or minor muscle strain — resolves on its own within two to four weeks. Staying active and using over-the-counter pain relief speeds recovery. Neck pain that persists beyond six weeks, keeps returning, or comes with arm symptoms should be assessed by a clinician.

faqs.question: Should I use a heat pack or ice for neck pain?
faqs.answer: Ice is most useful in the first 24 to 48 hours after an acute injury to reduce swelling and inflammation. Heat — a warm shower, heat pack, or warm towel — is more helpful after that phase and before physiotherapy or exercise, as it relaxes muscle spasm and improves tissue extensibility. Most people with neck muscle pain find heat more comfortable than ice.

faqs.question: Does an MRI always show the cause of neck pain?
faqs.answer: Not always. MRI frequently shows age-related disc and facet changes in people without any neck pain, and many people with significant neck pain have a normal or minimally changed MRI. For non-specific neck pain without neurological signs, MRI findings rarely change management and imaging is not routinely recommended at first presentation.

faqs.question: Can poor posture cause neck pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Forward-head posture from prolonged screen or phone use places up to twice the normal load on cervical muscles and joints with each centimetre of forward head displacement. Over time this fatigues the posterior neck muscles and accelerates facet joint and disc wear, producing both acute muscle pain and, eventually, degenerative cervicalgia.

faqs.question: What exercises help neck pain?
faqs.answer: The most evidence-supported exercises are deep neck flexor strengthening (chin tuck held for 10 seconds), cervical rotation and lateral flexion range-of-motion exercises, and scapular retraction. These address the most common deficits in recurrent neck pain. A physiotherapist can tailor a programme to the specific muscles involved in your case.

faqs.question: Can neck pain cause shoulder or arm pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cervical nerve root compression from a herniated disc or bone spur can cause pain, tingling, or numbness radiating from the neck down the arm and into the hand — cervical radiculopathy. Referred pain from cervical muscle trigger points and facet joints also commonly produces aching in the shoulders and upper back without nerve compression.

faqs.question: Is neck pain worse in the morning?
faqs.answer: Morning neck stiffness and pain that eases after 15 to 30 minutes of movement is typical of mechanical cervicalgia from overnight muscle loading. Morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, especially if it eases only after getting up and moving for a prolonged period, is a feature of inflammatory conditions such as ankylosing spondylitis and warrants investigation.

faqs.question: Should I wear a cervical collar for neck pain?
faqs.answer: A soft cervical collar is occasionally used for a day or two after acute injury for comfort, but prolonged collar use is not recommended because it leads to muscle deconditioning and delays recovery. Current guidelines for acute neck pain favour early movement and exercise over immobilisation except after significant trauma when instability must first be excluded.

faqs.question: When does neck pain need surgery?
faqs.answer: Surgery is considered when cervical radiculopathy causes arm weakness that does not improve after 6 to 12 weeks of conservative treatment, when imaging confirms the structural cause matches the clinical findings, or when cervical myelopathy threatens spinal cord function. The most common procedure is anterior cervical discectomy and fusion, which relieves arm pain in about 85% of patients.

faqs.question: Can stress cause neck pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Psychological stress activates muscle tension in the upper trapezius and posterior cervical muscles and lowers pain thresholds through central sensitisation, directly causing or worsening neck pain. Stress is one of the strongest predictors of transition from acute to chronic neck pain, which is why psychological approaches are part of evidence-based management for persistent cases.

faqs.question: What is the difference between neck pain and cervical radiculopathy?
faqs.answer: Neck pain (cervicalgia) is localised to the cervical region. Cervical radiculopathy adds radiation of pain, numbness, or tingling down the arm in a dermatomal pattern because a nerve root is compressed. Radiculopathy may also cause arm weakness or diminished reflexes, which distinguishes it from simple cervicalgia.

faqs.question: Does sleeping position affect neck pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sleeping prone (face down) forces the neck into prolonged rotation and extension and is consistently linked to neck pain. Side sleeping with a pillow that is too flat or too thick holds the neck in lateral flexion overnight. Back sleeping with a contoured cervical pillow that maintains the natural lordosis is generally best for people prone to neck pain.

faqs.question: Can physiotherapy cure neck pain?
faqs.answer: Physiotherapy is the most effective single treatment for chronic mechanical cervicalgia, with evidence showing 30 to 50% reduction in recurrence when exercise is continued. It does not cure structural degeneration, but it addresses the muscle imbalances and movement patterns that drive symptoms, enabling most people to manage and prevent flares effectively.

faqs.question: What is cervical spondylosis and how is it related to neck pain?
faqs.answer: Cervical spondylosis is the term for age-related wear on the discs and facet joints of the neck. Disc height loss, bone spur formation, and joint thickening gradually develop from the fourth decade onward. By age 65, radiological evidence is present in more than 85% of people and is the most common structural cause of both chronic neck pain and cervical nerve or cord compression.

metaDescription: Cervicalgia is neck pain from muscle strain, disc degeneration or nerve compression. Learn causes, red flags, physiotherapy, and when surgery is needed.
